topic_id: 'New Years Attack 2026': FBI Blocks Alleged Domestic ISIS Plot in NC
full_text: U.S. Attorney for the Western District of North Carolina Russ Ferguson announces charges against Christian Sturdivant, 18, for attempting to provide material support to a foreign terrorist organization. FIRST ON FOX - The FBI disrupted an alleged plot to attack people inside a grocery store and fast food restaurant in North Carolina on New Year's Eve in support of the Islamic State terrorist group, prosecutors revealed Friday. Christian Sturdivant, 18, of Mint Hill - a town outside Charlotte - was arrested and charged with attempting to provide material support to a foreign terrorist organization, U.S. Attorney for the Western District of North Carolina Russ Ferguson said. "The FBI and our partners continued working 24/7 over the holidays protecting the American people, and this case out of Charlotte foiling another alleged New Years Eve attacker is the latest example of their tremendous work," FBI Director Kash Patel said in a statement to Fox News Digital. "Our teams quickly identified the threat and acted decisively - no doubt saving American lives in the process. Thanks to our Joint Terrorism Task Force and regional partners both in New York and Western North Carolina for their efforts." GLOBAL WAVE OF TERROR PLOTS SPARKS NEW ALARMS OVER THE WEST'S GROWING VULNERABILITY Christian Sturdivant, the 18-year-old suspect from Mint Hill, N.C., who is accused of plotting an attack on New Year's Eve in support of ISIS. (Gaston County Sheriff's Office) Prosecutors said the alleged plot was foiled after Sturdivant recently began communicating online with an individual he "believed to be affiliated with ISIS but in reality was a government online covert employee," who is referred to in a criminal complaint as "OC." In one of the communications on Dec. 14, Sturdivant allegedly sent the OC an image of two hammers and a knife. "During his online communications with the OC, Sturdivant said, 'I will do jihad soon,' and proclaimed he was 'a soldier of the state,' meaning ISIS," the U.S. Attorney's Office said. "In subsequent online communications with the OC, Sturdivant indicated that he planned to attack a specific grocery store in North Carolina and discussed plans to purchase a firearm to use along with the knives during the attack," it added. Prosecutors said law enforcement seized from Sturdivant's bedroom "a blue hammer, a wooden-handled hammer, and two butcher knives hidden underneath the defendant's bed." On the right is a note titled, "New Years Attack 2026," that prosecutors said law enforcement found during a Dec. 29, 2025 search of Sturdivant's residence in Mint Hill, N.C. (United States Attorney's Office for the Western District of North Carolina) Ferguson said during a press conference on Friday that the attack was planned to take place in Mint Hill and the suspect worked at a Burger King. "He talked about where he was planning to do this attack, which was at a grocery store and a fast food restaurant in Mint Hill. Places that we go every day and don't think that we may be harmed," Ferguson added. " He was preparing for jihad, and innocent people were going to die. And we were very, very fortunate they did not," Ferguson also said, later adding, "I can tell you from his notes he was targeting, Jews, Christians and LGBTQ individuals." TEXAS MAN CHARGED WITH ATTEMPTING TO PROVIDE MATERIAL SUPPORT TO ISIS IN FEDERAL TERRORISM CASE Law enforcement seized from Sturdivant's bedroom a list of targets, as well as tactical gloves and a vest allegedly acquired as part of his planned attack. (United States Attorney's Office for the Western District of North Carolina) Attorney General Pam Bondi said in a statement that, "This successful collaboration between federal and local law enforcement saved American lives from a horrific terrorist attack on New Year's Eve." "The Department of Justice remains vigilant in our pursuit of evil ISIS sympathizers - anyone plotting to commit such depraved attacks will face the full force of the law," she continued. Prosecutors said a law enforcement search of Sturdivant's residence on Dec. 29 uncovered various handwritten documents, one of which was titled "New Years Attack 2026," and "listed, among other things, items such as a vest, mask, tactical gloves, and two knives allegedly to be used in the attack." "It also listed a goal of stabbing as many civilians as possible and [the] total number of victims as 20 to 21. The note also included a section listed as 'martyrdom Op,' with a plan to attack police that arrived at the site of the attack so the defendant would die a martyr," the Attorney's Office continued. "In addition, law enforcement seized from Sturdivant's bedroom a blue hammer, a wooden handled hammer, and two butcher knives hidden underneath the defendant's bed." Sturdivant remains held in federal custody, and if convicted, he faces a statutory maximum sentence of 20 years in federal prison, prosecutors said. "The FBI's mission is to protect the American people, and this case demonstrates our dedication to do everything we can to protect the residents of North Carolina. We worked closely with the Department of Justice, NYPD, Mint Hill Police Department, and our FBI Joint Terrorism Task Force partners to investigate and disrupt this serious threat of a violent attack. We will never stop working to hold people accountable who seek to harm and terrorize our community," said James C. Barnacle, Jr., special agent in charge of the FBI's Charlotte Field Office. Sturdivant allegedly posted an image in early December depicting two miniature figurines of Jesus with the on-screen text that included "May Allah curse the cross worshipers," according to court documents. (United States Attorney's Office for the Western District of North Carolina) The Attorney's Office, citing the criminal complaint, said on Dec. 18, the FBI in Charlotte "received information that an individual later identified as Sturdivant was making multiple social media posts in support of ISIS, a designated foreign terrorist organization." "In one social post in early December 2025, Sturdivant posted an image depicting two miniature figurines of Jesus with the on-screen text that included 'May Allah curse the cross worshipers.' The post is allegedly consistent with ISIS historic practice calling for the extermination of all non-believers, including Christians and other Muslims who do not agree with the terrorist organization's extreme ideology," the Attorney's Office added. Barnacle said at a press conference on Friday that Sturdivant first came to the FBI's radar in January 2022, when he was still a juvenile. "He was in contact via social media with an unidentified ISIS member overseas. Sturdivant received direction from this unidentified ISIS member to dress in all black, knock on people's doors, and attack them with a hammer. In fact, Sturdivant did dress in all black. He left this house with a hammer, and fortunately his family stepped in," Barnacle continued. "No charges were filed at that time. He was referred for psychological care, and he underwent psychological care." "This investigation highlights the very real threat posed by people who self-radicalized online and are inspired by jihadist ideologies espoused by foreign terrorist organizations," Barnacle also said. " We rely on the communities we serve and encourage the public to contact law enforcement when they see or hear something that doesn't seem right. I also have this personal message - if your child, relative, friend, or neighbor is sliding into a dangerous ideology, you'll be the first to see it. And together we can stop it. The threats we face are bigger than any one law enforcement or intelligence agency, requiring everyone who hears this message to work together to keep people safe," he added. topic_id: Mamdani Sworn in As NYC Mayor
full_text: Mayor Zohran Mamdani acknowledged skeptics of his plans while reiterating that he would focus on working-class New Yorkers. By Jeffery C. Mays and Emma G. Fitzsimmons Mayor Zohran Mamdani gave a soaring inaugural address on Thursday in front of thousands of New Yorkers and political luminaries including Senator Bernie Sanders and Representative Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, laying out what he called audacious plans to govern the city with a focus on working-class New Yorkers. The ascension of Mr. Mamdani, a 34-year-old democratic socialist, to New York City's top job marked a number of firsts. He is the first Muslim and South Asian person to serve as mayor, the first to use a Quran at his swearing-in and the youngest mayor in over a century. Mr. Mamdani highlighted many of those points in his speech, which celebrated the diversity, food and culture of New York City. The event was filled with symbolism, including the traditional labor movement song " Bread and Roses ," performed by the musician Lucy Dacus, which focuses on the rights of workers to justice and dignity. Now the countdown begins as he sets out to fulfill his lofty promises to "deliver an agenda of safety, affordability and abundance - where government looks and lives like the people it represents." As Mr. Mamdani noted, those goals will not be easily achieved. Here are seven takeaways from Mr. Mamdani's inauguration. In one of his speech's most dramatic moments, Mr. Mamdani declared that he would govern the way he campaigned - as a democratic socialist - and said that meant he would focus on working-class New Yorkers, regardless of criticism from naysayers. "I was elected as a democratic socialist, and I will govern as a democratic socialist," he said. "I will not abandon my principles for fear of being deemed radical." An earlier version of this article misstated the last time Senator Bernie Sanders swore in a mayor of New York City. It was 2018, not 2017. Jeffery C. Mays is a Times reporter covering politics with a focus on New York City Hall. Emma G. Fitzsimmons is the City Hall bureau chief for The Times, covering Mayor Eric Adams and his administration.

topic_id: Mamdani Sworn in As NYC Mayor
full_text: NYC Councilwoman Vickie Paladino criticizes newly sworn-in Mayor Zohran Mamdani's vow to replace individualism with collectivism, warning his socialist policies will face strong opposition. Freshly inaugurated New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani, a self-described Democratic socialist, promoted "collectivism" over "rugged individualism" during a speech on Thursday, and prominent conservative figures nationwide responded by sounding the alarm about collectivist ideology. "We will replace the frigidity of rugged individualism with the warmth of collectivism," Mamdani declared in his inaugural address. Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis - a Republican who pursued the GOP presidential nomination during the past election cycle but ultimately dropped out and endorsed President Donald Trump - raised the matter of the massive death toll associated with collectivism. HOURS AFTER TAKING OFFICE, NYC MAYOR MAMDANI TARGETS LANDLORDS, MOVES TO INTERVENE IN PRIVATE BANKRUPTCY CASE "The 'warmth' of collectivism that always requires coercion and force. How many dead over the past 100 years due to collectivist ideologies?" DeSantis wrote in a post on X. House Republican Conference Chair Rep. Lisa McClain of Michigan called Mamdani a "communist," a label which he has previously rejected. DESANTIS WARNS GOP FACES 'ISSUE' KEEPING TRUMP VOTERS ENGAGED IN FUTURE ELECTIONS "Zohran Mamdani is a dangerous communist who is likely to DESTROY NYC through his dedication to communist ideology. topic_id: Trump Warns Iran Following Reports of Deadly Protests
full_text: US president says Washington will intervene if Tehran violently suppresses protests over high prices. United States President Donald Trump has said Washington is "locked and loaded" to respond if Iran kills more protesters, after cost-of-living demonstrations in the country turned deadly. Protesters and security forces clashed in several Iranian cities on Thursday, with six reported killed in the first deaths since the unrest escalated. Shopkeepers in the capital, Tehran, went on strike on Sunday over high prices and economic stagnation, actions that have since spread to other parts of the country. Trump said on his Truth Social platform on Friday that "if Iran shoots and violently kills peaceful protesters, which is their custom, the United States of America will come to their rescue. "We are locked and loaded and ready to go," the Republican leader added. Ali Larijani, secretary of the Supreme National Security Council, condemned Trump's remarks, saying he "should know that American interference in this internal issue is equivalent to chaos across the entire region and the destruction of American interests". "We consider the positions of the protesting merchants separate from those of the destructive elements," Larijani added in a post on X. "The people of the US should know that Trump began the adventurism. They should take care of their own soldiers." Larijani's remarks likely referenced the US's wide military footprint in the region. In June, Iran attacked Al Udeid airbase in Qatar after the US strikes on three Iranian nuclear sites during Israel's 12-day war with Iran. Iran's Fars news agency reported on Thursday that two people were killed in clashes between security forces and protesters in the city of Lordegan, in the province of Chaharmahal and Bakhtiari, and three in Azna, in neighbouring Lorestan province. State television reported earlier that a member of Iran's security forces was killed overnight during protests in the western city of Kuhdasht. Dozens of arrests were also reported by the authorities across multiple cities. Protests also took place on Friday in several cities including Qom, Marvdasht, Yasuj, Mashhad, Hamadan and eastern parts of Tehran, Iranian media reported. Al Jazeera's Tohid Asadi, reporting from Tehran, said Iran's economic woes are putting significant pressure on Iranians. "These protests initially started sporadically, but later they have spread into smaller cities across," he said. "We can see this public mood and dissatisfaction in the protests," he said, adding that the confrontations between the protesters and security forces mainly happened in smaller cities. Iran's economy has struggled for years since the US reimposed sanctions in 2018, after Trump withdrew from an international nuclear agreement during his first term. The deal was agreed by his predecessor Barack Obama after a long string of negotiations involving Iran and other Western powers. The latest protests are the biggest since 2022, when unrest was triggered by the death in custody of Mahsa Amini, who was arrested for allegedly violating Iran's strict dress code for women. Her death prompted a nationwide wave of anger that left several hundred people dead, including dozens of members of the security forces.

topic_id: Trump Warns Iran Following Reports of Deadly Protests
full_text: Iran's Foreign Ministry warned Friday that the United States would bear "full responsibility for the consequences" if it intervenes in ongoing protests inside the country, escalating tensions as demonstrations continue. This follows Trump and Tehran's top security official hurling warnings early Friday, escalating a crisis already marked by deadly clashes. "If Iran shots [sic] and violently kills peaceful protesters, which is their custom, the United States of America will come to their rescue," the U.S. president wrote on Truth Social. "We are locked and loaded and ready to go. Thank you for your attention to this matter!" Unrest has erupted across Iran as protests over soaring inflation and the collapse of the national currency escalated into violent clashes with security forces. Rights groups and local media report multiple deaths in several provinces, marking the most serious wave of demonstrations in three years. The news cycle is loud. Algorithms push us to extremes. The unrest has drawn international attention, with U.S. President Donald Trump warning that America is "locked and loaded" if Iran kills peaceful protesters. Israel's foreign ministry posted messages in Farsi praising demonstrators as "lions and lionesses." Speculation about further strikes on Iran has grown after last year's 12-day war, during which U.S. and Israeli forces targeted Iranian nuclear facilities. Pezeshkian responded that any aggression would be met with a "harsh and discouraging" reply, underscoring the risk of escalation. The current protests recall mass demonstrations in 2022 after the death of Mahsa Amini in police custody. Then, women removed headscarves in defiance of the morality police, sparking nationwide unrest. Security forces responded with mass arrests and live fire, leaving hundreds dead. A UN investigation later described the crackdown as "crimes against humanity," a charge Tehran rejected. While dress code enforcement has since eased, the memory of Amini's death continues to inspire calls for freedom and justice. Former United States Secretary of State Mike Pompeo has warned that the Iranian regime "is in trouble" as the protests that have left at least six dead continue. " The Iranian regime is in trouble. Bringing in mercenaries is its last best hope," he wrote on X, after President Donald Trump also warned that any further violence towards protesters would result in direct action from the U.S. "Riots in dozens of cities and the Basij under siege - Mashed, Tehran, Zahedan. Next stop: Baluchistan. 47 years of this regime; POTUS 47. Coincidence? Happy New Year to every Iranian in the streets. Also to every Mossad agent walking beside them..." President Pezeshkian's administration has avoided a full-scale crackdown, signaling openness to "legitimate demands." The government replaced the central bank governor and pledged to stabilize the currency. Campus security managers at major universities were removed after misconduct during student protests. At a ceremony marking the anniversary of Qassem Soleimani's death, Pezeshkian vowed to fight corruption and protect livelihoods, calling them a "red line." Analysts say his cautious approach contrasts with past crackdowns but faces resistance from entrenched interests. What began with shopkeepers in Tehran's Grand Bazaar has spread to 17 provinces, drawing students and workers into the streets. At least seven people have been killed and dozens arrested in clashes with security forces. In Lordegan, protesters attacked government buildings, prompting police to use tear gas. Similar unrest was reported in Azna, Kouhdasht, and Fasa, where demonstrators set fire to state offices. The scale of mobilization suggests growing frustration that has moved beyond economic grievances into political dissent. Farah Pahlavi, the former Queen and last Empress of Iran, said she had "full faith" that Iranians could reclaim "your land, your freedom and your dignity." "For 47 years, you have endured unimaginable hardships, watching your beautiful country fall under the control of an oppressive regime that has denied you your rights, your dignity and your prosperity," she wrote in a post on X on Friday, as protests rolled on for another day. "But today, I see the spirit of Iran rising stronger than ever. I see the youth, the men and women from every corner of this great land, standing up and speaking out with a united voice that can no longer be ignored. Your courage, your strength and your resilience fill my heart with pride. You are not alone in your struggle. The eyes of the world are upon you and your call for freedom and justice resonates beyond the borders of Iran. "Do not fear, for the darkness of oppression cannot last forever. You are the true heart and soul of this nation and your hopes as well as your desire for change cannot be extinguished. The road ahead will be difficult, and the challenges are great. The pain of these years is real, and the consequences of oppression have been deep, but your will is greater than any obstacle and your love for your country will lead you to victory. "We are a people with a proud history of culture, art, intellect and innovation. Iran was once a beacon of hope and progress and it will be again. The future belongs to those who dare to dream, who dare to fight for a better tomorrow. I have full faith that you will reclaim your land, your freedom and your dignity. To my fellow Iranians, hold onto your faith in each other. Stand together, with love in your hearts for this beautiful land and for the generations yet to come. Your struggle is not in vain. This moment is yours. This time will be remembered as the turning point, when the Iranian people took their future back into their hands. I call on the security forces to join the people in this movement. Light will prevail over darkness." Minister of Diaspora Affairs of Israel Amichai Chikli drew a sharp contrast between U.S. President Donald Trump and former President Barack Obama in a post on X. Writing in Hebrew, Chikli described Trump as "the leader of the free world" while accusing Obama of policies that, in his view, strengthened Iran, empowered the Muslim Brotherhood in Egypt, and destabilized the Middle East. The United Nations Office of Human Rights has said it is closely following the ongoing protests, and reports of violence, in Iran "with concern." "I call on the authorities to uphold the rights to freedom of expression, association and peaceful assembly. All individuals must be able to protest peacefully and express their grievances," U.N. High Commissioner Volker Turk added. Jeb Bush, the brother of George W. Bush, praised Donald Trump for his "historic leadership on Iran" after the president warned the country that the U.S. was "locked and loaded and ready to go," if Iran killed any of its protesters. "The Islamic Republic does not represent the great Iranian people. President Trump continues to demonstrate historic leadership on Iran," Bush said on X on Friday. Unrest has erupted across Iran as protests over soaring inflation and the collapse of the national currency escalated into violent clashes with security forces. Rights groups and local media report multiple deaths in several provinces, marking the most serious wave of demonstrations in three years. Carl Bildt, the co-chair of the European Council on Foreign Relations and former Swedish Prime Minister, warned on Friday that the protests in Iran were "getting serious." "Street protests in Iran are getting serious, triggered by collapse of the currency and inflation, but also with political slogans in the traditionally important bazaar of Teheran," he wrote on X, alongside several videos showing Iranians taking to the streets, tearing down gates, and setting fires. United Nations High Commissioner for Human Rights Volker Turk voiced concern Friday over reports of violence during ongoing protests in Iran. "We're following the ongoing protests, and reports of violence, with concern," Turk said, urging authorities to safeguard fundamental freedoms. He called on officials to uphold the rights to freedom of expression, association, and peaceful assembly. Actor Michael Rapaport has called for more support for Iranians protesting against their brutal regime. The star, who has appeared in Friends and Prison Break , said in a post on X that while he had seen "endless" protests on behalf of Gaza, he was yet to see activists supporting Iranians. "Where the f--k is everybody on Iran? I've seen endless protests for Gaza. Screaming. Chanting. Blocking streets. Harassing people. Destroying careers. People willing to risk everything - jobs, reputations, safety - supposedly in the name of the "oppressed." But Iran? Crickets. Where's the outrage for the Iranian people living under a brutal theocratic regime?" he said in a post on New Year's Eve. "Where's the rage for women who don't have basic rights? For girls beaten, jailed, or killed for not wearing a hijab "correctly"? For journalists, artists, LGBTQ people, dissidents - silenced, tortured, disappeared? Nothing. No marches. No tents. No screaming into megaphones. No viral chants." Teacher unions and labor groups across Iran have publicly endorsed the country's sixth day of protests, warning that economic hardship, repression, and inequality have driven society toward open confrontation with authorities, Iran International reports. Statements of support came from teachers' associations in Mazandaran and Fars provinces, as well as Eslamshahr and Nurabad Mamasani. The Mazandaran teachers' union used stark language, describing a population forced into protest by "hunger, repression and loss of dignity." Labor organizations, including the Free Union of Iranian Workers and the Tehran and Suburbs Bus Workers' Syndicate, also backed strikes and demonstrations, urging nationwide unity. Analysts say the involvement of organized teachers and workers signals grievances that extend beyond wages, pointing to systemic injustice, shrinking livelihoods, and sustained crackdowns that have transformed professional demands into a broader social challenge to the Islamic Republic. The social media account for 2023 Nobel Peace Prize laureate Narges Mohammadi has shared a call from 17 Iranian activists for security forces not to carry out their "orders of repression." "After decades of squandering and destroying national resources in pursuit of its adventurist and bankrupt policies, the Islamic Republic has turned inflation, poverty, insecurity, corruption, and injustice into tools of repression and means to wear down and humiliate the people," the statement posted on X stated. "Yet once again, the great movement of civil resistance by the people of Iran, by taking over the streets, is proclaiming the national will to remove the illegitimate regime of the Islamic Republic. We stand with the people to reclaim the right to a dignified life, freedom, justice, human dignity, and sovereignty over our own destiny." "We warn the law-enforcement and security forces to remain faithful to their true responsibility-protecting the lives and security of the people of Iran; not carrying out orders of repression, not standing against the nation, and not shedding the blood of defenseless citizens. Any violence against the people constitutes direct participation in a crime that will be recorded in the historical memory of this land. Today is a time for choice: stand with the nation and separate your ranks from the repressors who are on their way out and must be held accountable. We reaffirm once more that the only path to saving Iran is a transition away from the Islamic Republic-a demand that is neither temporary nor suppressible. The future of Iran will rest on a democratic order grounded in popular sovereignty, national interests, and normal relations with all countries of the world," he added. Ali Akbar Raefipour, a conservative Iranian commentator, urged supporters to launch a social media campaign targeting U.S. President Donald Trump following his recent remarks on Iran's protests. In a post translated from Persian, Raefipour described Trump's comments as "a declaration of war against our dear Iran" and called on Iranians to unite under the hashtag #ShutUpTrump. Relatives of those killed in the 2020 downing of Ukraine International Airlines flight PS752 by Iran's Revolutionary Guards have expressed solidarity with ongoing protests across the country. In a statement Friday, the Association of Families of PS752 Victims condemned the use of live fire, mass arrests, and violent repression against demonstrators, calling strikes and market closures lawful civic acts. The group said protesters are demanding the overthrow of the Islamic Republic, not merely reforms to address corruption or mismanagement. News of recent protester deaths has deepened the families' grief ahead of the sixth anniversary of the PS752 tragedy on January 8, they added. Drawing on their own experience, the families warned that denial of truth and impunity for perpetrators only pave the way for further disasters. They urged authorities to halt violent crackdowns, release detainees, and ensure transparent accountability for those killed or injured, stressing that human lives must not be the cost of state misrule. Iranian diplomat Seyed Abbas Araghchi responded sharply to U.S. President Donald Trump's recent comments on unrest in Iran, calling them "reckless and dangerous." In a post on X, Araghchi acknowledged that citizens affected by exchange rate volatility have the right to protest peacefully but distinguished those demonstrations from what he described as "isolated violent riots," including attacks on police stations and Molotov cocktails thrown at officers. He argued that Trump, who has deployed the National Guard within U.S. borders, should understand that "criminal attacks on public property cannot be tolerated." Araghchi added that the Iranian people will "forcefully reject any interference in their internal affairs" and warned that Iran's armed forces are prepared to respond decisively to any violation of its sovereignty. Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene has criticized President Donald Trump for threatening to send troops into Iran, saying it is "everything we voted against in 2024." Greene, a former Trump ally turned critic, was speaking after the president warned Iran that the U.S. was "locked and loaded and ready to go." "An Israeli cybersecurity billionaire demanding to take away Americans guaranteed First Amendment Free Speech and President Trump threatening war and sending in troops to Iran is everything we voted against in '24," she wrote on X on Friday. "Trump voters spent the week threatening a tax revolt because they are so furious about the never ending waste, fraud, and abuse of their hard earned money going to foreigners and foreign wars. The focus should be on tax dollars here at home and defending our God given freedoms and rights." Exiled opposition figure Reza Pahlavi issued a message of support to demonstrators across Iran, hailing them as "noble and brave compatriots" and urging continued solidarity in the face of government crackdowns. In a post shared on X, Pahlavi listed provinces from Lorestan and Isfahan to Tehran and Khorasan, saying protesters were "making history" through courage and determination. He honored those killed in recent clashes, calling them "true champions of this land" whose names would live on in national memory. Pahlavi urged Iranians to commemorate the victims of the uprising both at home and abroad, framing their struggle as part of a collective path toward freedom. "Stay united. Stay focused on the goal. Victory is ours," he wrote, closing his message with "Long live Iran." His remarks highlight the symbolic role of exiled opposition leaders in amplifying the voices of protesters demanding change inside the country. Republican politician Kari Lake amplified U.S. President Donald Trump's warning to Tehran, posting on X that "America stands with the Iranian People." Quoting Trump's phrase "locked and loaded," Lake thanked him for his stance and urged demonstrators to "be strong." Ali Larijani, secretary of Iran's Supreme National Security Council, issued a sharp warning to Washington following President Donald Trump's comments on ongoing unrest in Iran. In a post on X, Larijani said that "with the stances taken by Israeli officials and Trump, the behind-the-scenes of the matter has become clear," accusing the United States of adventurism. He distinguished between protesting merchants and what he called "destructive elements." Still, he cautioned that any American involvement in the demonstrations would bring "chaos across the entire region and the destruction of American interests." Larijani added that U.S. citizens should recognize Trump as the source of escalation and "look after their soldiers." Iran's latest wave of protests, sparked by economic collapse and spreading nationwide, has been defined by powerful chants that reveal both frustration with government policies and calls for broader change. Early demonstrations echoed with "Not Gaza, not Lebanon, may my life be sacrificed for Iran," a slogan criticizing Tehran's regional alliances with militant groups. At the same time, domestic inflation and currency depreciation devastate livelihoods. Shopkeepers and students have joined forces, with one young woman in Tehran's Grand Bazaar rallying crowds by shouting, "Don't be afraid! We are all together!"-a moment that underscored unity across social divides. University campuses remain flashpoints, where chants of "Freedom! Freedom! Freedom!" and "Iranians, speak up and demand your rights!" have spread despite heavy surveillance. Protesters have also directed appeals toward security forces, chanting "Police officers! Support us!" in hopes of shifting loyalties. Anger at Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei has been explicit, with cries of "Death to the dictator" and "This year will be the year of blood, Ali will be overthrown." Some demonstrators have invoked Iran's deposed monarchy, chanting "Reza Shah, may your soul rest in peace," while others reject both past and present systems with slogans like "Neither Shah nor Mullah." Over the past few years, unrest and protests have erupted across Iran, with various demands against the government, including a notable period of demonstrations in 2009 known as the Green Movement. The 2009 Iranian presidential election saw unprecedented voter turnout, mainly due to the excitement over reformist candidates and the thought of change within the system. Following the election, many took to the streets to dispute the count that put hardliner Mahmoud Ahmadinejad in power. At the time many Iranians chanted in Farsi, "Where is my vote?" However, while protests today also express frustration with the Iranian government, experts have noted there isn't a clear, unified political demand or direction, rather just overwhelming discontent with a slew of government policies, both foreign and domestic. When asked about the comparison of 2009, 2018 protests, and the most recent 2022 Mahsa Amini protests to today, "There are different and similar at the same time," Vali Nasr, professor of Middle East Studies and International Affairs at Johns Hopkins School of Advanced International Studies told Newsweek. He noted that the instigation of the protests are different, from alleged voter fraud, to morality police and hijab policies, to the economy, as well as some of the participants involved. For Nader Hashemi, a Georgetown University professor, "Today's protests are different than previous protests in the sense that they directly impact the lives of every single Iranian citizen," he said in an email to Newsweek. " When the value of the currency drops by 56% in less than six months and inflation exceeds 50% this translates into mass poverty and pauperization for millions of Iranians (who have already been living in tough economic and political conditions). In this sense, the protests are not driven by a single group of people (women, youth, working class or ethnic minorities) but they are broad-based and widely felt. Everyone has a reason to protest," he added. Nasr, a senior fellow at the Middle East Institute, and Alex Vatanka noted that as the protestors have no clear political directive, it's unclear how long the protests will continue and what their demands are outside of economic relief. There isn't "a singular opposition leader to rally around," Vatanka said, noting that in previous protests, such as the Green Movement, Iranians who were protesting were rallying around specific figures such as Hossein Moussavi or Mehdi Karroubi. "Nobody's shouting slogans in favor of anyone in Iran to the extent that I know ," adding that the slogans are against the Islamic Republic and leaders like Ayatollah Ali Khamenei. While shopkeepers first took to the streets in a protest of the country's poor economic conditions, Iranians from across economic classes and regions have joined in the mass protests. Fueling them all is an anger towards the government and the country's mismanagement of resources and continual economic crises as inflation continues to spike, the currency has fallen, and the cost of living is largely unmanageable. Senior fellow at the Middle East Institute, Alex Vatanka, described the mood of the protest movement to Newsweek in phone interview Friday as "not about hope, but about a sense of fatalism," adding that "hope is secondary." He painted a bleak picture of the state of affairs, saying Iranians are "so exacerbated," "so tired," and "don't see a way out of this." Vali Nasr, professor of Middle East Studies and International Affairs at Johns Hopkins School of Advanced International Studies, noted, "I think partly the anger reflects the absence of hope." He continued that the protestors lack of political unity and direction, as well as the fact that there "is not a major crack in the regime that they could rally around," depicts a "sense of despondency, more than hope in the protests right now." Iran's week-long protests, sparked by economic despair and widening into calls for regime change, have left multiple demonstrators dead, according to human rights groups. The Center for Human Rights in Iran reported six fatalities by Friday, while the Hengaw Organization for Human Rights said eight people had been killed, including a 15-year-old. U.S. President Donald Trump warned on social media that if Iran "violently kills peaceful protesters," the United States would "come to their rescue," adding that America was "locked and loaded." Shortly after, Ali Larijani, secretary of Iran's Supreme National Security Council, responded on X that U.S. interference would bring "chaos in the entire region" and harm American interests. Iranian authorities have acknowledged only one death, claiming Amirhesam Khodayarifard was a Basij militia member. However, rights groups say he was a protester and allege officials pressured his family to adopt the state narrative. Additional deaths were reported in Lordegan and Fouladshahr, though authorities disputed some accounts. Videos verified by the BBC showed protesters in Lorestan setting fire to a police station. The unrest, initially triggered by the collapse of the rial, has spread nationwide, with chants demanding fundamental change and even praising Iran's deposed monarchy. A phrase used by U.S. President Donald Trump in support of Iranian protesters carries a specific military meaning, analysts say , suggesting more than political rhetoric. International relations scholar Kamran Matin told Iran International that Trump's wording amounted to an explicit threat, potentially signaling readiness for military action. Matin noted that the scope of Trump's remarks appeared to extend beyond Iran's missile program or regional activities, encompassing the government's violent response to domestic protests. He cautioned, however, that Trump's personal style-marked by shifting positions and ambiguous statements-allows for multiple interpretations. While U.S. and Israeli forces have shown signs of military preparedness in the region, Matin stressed that verbal threats do not always translate into action. In 2009, during Barack Obama's first term as president, protests broke out in Iran following the election of president Mahmoud Ahmadinejad. The Ayatollah Khamenei called the protests "unacceptable" and responded by violently suppressing them, with protesters beaten, arrested and tortured, and even shot. When asked about the violent suppression, then-President Obama warned that "the world is watching." "What you're seeing in Iran are hundreds of thousands of people who believe their voices were not heard and who are peacefully protesting and seeking justice. And the world is watching," he said in June 2009. He added that he was "y concerned, based on some of the tenor and tone of the statements that have been made" by the Iranian government but that he hoped "that the world understands that this is not something that has to do with the outside world; this has to do with what's happening in Iran. And I think ultimately the Iranian people will obtain justice." Mohsen Rezaei, a senior Iranian official and former commander of the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, issued a stark warning on X in response to U.S. President Donald Trump's criticism of Tehran over the killing of protesters. Rezaei cautioned that any "hostile" action against Iran would destroy Israel, U.S. bases, and regional stability. His remarks underscore Tehran's defiant posture amid mounting international condemnation of its crackdown on demonstrations. Amnesty International expressed alarm over reports that protesters have been killed during ongoing unrest in Iran, urging authorities to respect fundamental rights. In a statement shared by its Iran account, the organization called on officials to uphold freedom of expression and peaceful assembly and to refrain from using unlawful force. Iranian activist and journalist Masih Alinejad has called on U.S. President Donald Trump to move beyond verbal support and take concrete steps to aid protesters in Iran. In a post on X, she thanked Trump for his "strong support," but stressed that "your words matter" only if backed by immediate measures. Alinejad outlined three actions: expanding free internet access by asking Elon Musk to increase Starlink coverage, expelling Iranian diplomats from the United States, and imposing "maximum consequences" on officials who ordered live fire against demonstrators. She argued that representatives of a government accused of shooting protesters should not enjoy diplomatic privileges and insisted that those responsible must face "real, personal, and irreversible consequences." Iran's Chief Justice of Lorestan Province has ordered a judicial investigation following the death of Amir Hesam Khodayarifard during clashes in Kuhdasht on December 30, 2026. In an urgent directive addressed to local judicial authorities, Hojjatoleslam Saeed Shahvari expressed regret over the incident and called for a thorough legal inquiry. He demanded a "decisive and unlenient confrontation" with those responsible, stressing that no tolerance would be acceptable. Shahvari instructed the Prosecutor of Kuhdasht to establish a special branch, conduct field investigations, and collect evidence to ensure the case is pursued to its conclusion. He emphasized that the judiciary must take all legal measures to uncover the truth and strengthen public trust by implementing justice. Separately, the head of the Fasa County Judiciary denied reports that a person had been killed during an attack on the Fasa Governorate. Protests across Iran have entered their sixth day, spreading from Tehran to cities nationwide with chants openly calling for regime change. Demonstrators have rallied in major urban centers, defying security forces despite escalating crackdowns. Authorities have responded with live fire, killing at least seven protesters so far, according to reports. The unrest, initially sparked by economic grievances, has intensified into a direct challenge to the Islamic Republic's leadership. A map of the protesting cities shows the breadth of the movement. Protests across Iran that began over soaring inflation, and the rising cost of living have escalated into a broader rejection of the Islamic Republic, with demonstrators describing a society pushed to a psychological breaking point. "Refrigerators are empty. Pockets are empty... Every day, people see themselves becoming poorer," one protester told the Jerusalem Post . Another described Iran's economy as monopolistic and dominated by elites tied to the regime and the Revolutionary Guards, saying "the entire economy has been swallowed." Iranian Defense Minister Aziz Nasirzadeh declared that the country's military strength has advanced "far beyond those of the 12-Day War," warning that any threat against the Islamic Republic would be met with a "complete and decisive" response. His remarks were broadcast by state television IRIB. Nasirzadeh also defended Iran's missile program, insisting U.S. military action or diplomatic negotiations cannot dismantle it. "Bombs or negotiations cannot destroy Iran's missile capability," he said, underscoring Tehran's view of its missile arsenal as a cornerstone of national defense. Israel's Persian-language social media account posted a message praising demonstrators in Iran, describing them as "lion-men and lion-women" rising to battle against darkness. The statement, translated from Persian, declared that "light is victorious over darkness." As protests intensify, Iranian leaders have vowed to continue rebuilding the country's ballistic missile program despite U.S. and Israeli concerns. President Donald Trump suggested Washington could support Israeli strikes on Iran's missile facilities, but Iranian officials rejected concessions, calling missile capabilities a cornerstone of national defense. Ali Shamkhani, a senior adviser, warned that Iran would respond to threats before they materialize. Analysts say Tehran's defiance reflects its reliance on missiles as a deterrent, even as domestic unrest challenges the regime's stability. Molavi Abdolhamid Ismailzehi, a prominent Sunni cleric from Iran's Baloch community, has sharply criticized the country's Shia leadership, saying citizens' lives and livelihoods have reached a dead end amid ongoing unrest. In recent remarks, Abdolhamid defended the right of Iranians to hold peaceful demonstrations and called on security forces to avoid violence against protesters. He stressed that officials must listen to public grievances and pursue reforms to address widespread dissatisfaction. Protests that began in Tehran's Grand Bazaar have now spread to cities across Iran, including Karaj, Hamedan, Qeshm, Malard, Isfahan, Kermanshah, Shiraz, and Yazd. The unrest was triggered when shopkeepers went on strike after the Iranian rial plunged to a record low against the U.S. dollar. Videos verified by BBC Persian show demonstrators taking to the streets as police deployed tear gas in an attempt to disperse crowds. What started as an economic protest over inflation and currency collapse has quickly escalated into a broader wave of dissent. Iran's foreign ministry spokesperson Esmaeil Baghaei dismissed warnings from U.S. President Donald Trump over the country's handling of protests, insisting that Iranians will resolve their own problems without outside involvement. "Iranians, through dialogue and interaction with one another to resolve their problems, will not permit any form of foreign interference," Baghaei wrote in a post on X as demonstrations entered their sixth consecutive day. Iran International reported it has verified the death of 22-year-old Ahmad Jalil, who was shot and killed by security forces during demonstrations in the town of Lordegan on Thursday. Jalil, a ride-hailing driver struggling financially, had previously joined the 2022 "Woman, Life, Freedom" protests, according to relatives. His family told Iran International that intelligence agents from the Revolutionary Guards summoned them to the governor's office, threatening not to release his body unless they recorded a coerced interview blaming protesters for his death. Sources also alleged that authorities have pressured and financially coerced families of those killed in recent unrest, seeking to suppress accountability and control public narratives. Demonstrators in the southeastern city of Zahedan took to the streets on Friday, following prayers and chanted slogans including "Death to Khamenei," according to Iran International. Iranian activist and journalist Masih Alinejad has called on U.S. President Donald Trump to move beyond words and take concrete steps in support of demonstrators in Iran. In a post on X, she thanked Trump for what she described as strong backing but insisted immediate action was needed. Alinejad urged expanded access to free internet, appealing to Elon Musk to boost Starlink coverage so authorities cannot suppress protests by cutting connectivity. She also pressed Washington to expel Iranian diplomats, arguing that representatives of a government accused of shooting protesters should not enjoy diplomatic privileges. Calling for "maximum consequences," Alinejad said officials who ordered live fire must face "real, personal, and irreversible consequences." Her remarks highlight growing demands from activists for international measures that go beyond statements of solidarity as unrest continues inside Iran. Alireza Salimi, a member of Iran's parliamentary presidium, issued a sharp warning to Washington on Friday following remarks by U.S. President Donald Trump about Iran's handling of ongoing protests. "Trump the gambler should know that our response to any action or interference will be regret-inducing," Salimi said, according to Iranian media. Kentucky Congressman Thomas Massie voiced opposition to potential U.S. military strikes on Iran, arguing that domestic priorities should take precedence over foreign intervention. In a post on X, Massie said the United States "shouldn't be wasting military resources on another country's internal affairs." He emphasized that any military action against Iran would require congressional authorization, underscoring constitutional limits on executive power. Massie further contended that the threat was not about freedom of speech in Iran but tied to "the dollar, oil, and Israel." Protests swept across Iran this week as soaring inflation and a collapsing currency deepened public frustration with the government. Demonstrations that began among business owners and university students in major cities quickly spread to smaller towns, with reports of clashes and injuries among both protesters and security forces. Iran's currency fell to a record low against the U.S. dollar last weekend, compounding the strain of annual inflation that reached 42.2 percent in December. The economic crisis has eroded purchasing power and pushed many Iranians into hardship, fueling anger at officials seen as unable to stabilize the situation. Authorities acknowledged injuries among security officers and confirmed at least one fatality, though independent verification has been difficult. Human rights groups reported additional deaths in Lordegan, a western city where protests turned violent on Thursday. Unlike past uprisings, where authorities relied heavily on force, senior leaders this time have signaled a willingness to engage with protest organizers. Officials have floated the possibility of dialogue, though skepticism remains high among demonstrators who recall previous crackdowns. The unrest comes as Iran faces mounting external pressure, including U.S. sanctions and renewed threats from Washington. President Donald Trump's vow to intervene if protesters are killed has added a volatile international dimension to what began as a domestic economic crisis. President Massoud Pezeshkian's call for "unity" and "national reconciliation" is increasingly viewed as a political trap, with critics arguing that his conciliatory posture has yielded gains for hardliners while leaving moderates disillusioned. Since taking office, Pezeshkian has softened his rhetoric, appointed rivals to key posts, and defended compromise as necessary for stability. Reformist daily Sharq warned last week that reconciliation had become "a one-way road," noting that hardline lawmakers privately acknowledge the need for fuel price hikes but publicly posture as defenders of the poor. Analysts say hardliners have used Pezeshkian's overtures to reclaim influence and advance policies at odds with his campaign pledges, from internet restrictions to strict enforcement of hijab. Even explicit backing from the Supreme Leader has not shielded the president, as parliamentarians amplify public anxiety and push prosecutions that weaken institutions. Rouydad24 questioned Pezeshkian's claims of resistance, pointing to his appointment of Saghab Esfahani as vice president for energy consumption optimization as evidence of retreat. Columnist Zohreh Farahani argued that governance requires courage and accountability-qualities absent in the current administration. The result, reformists contend, is a deepening deadlock: reconciliation has flowed in only one direction, leaving Pezeshkian weakened, moderates frustrated, and Iran's entrenched power balance unchanged. A series of rare videos posted by Iranian police officers describing severe financial hardship has sparked widespread reaction online, raising questions about morale within the country's security forces. The first clip, filmed in Yasuj, featured Staff Sergeant Mohammad-Amin Ardeshir-Moghaddam lamenting low wages and admitting he was considering selling a kidney to cover living costs. Days later, he retracted the remarks, calling them a "heart-to-heart talk" with the national police chief, fueling speculation that the reversal came under pressure. Soon after, Third Lieutenant Mostafa Loghmani in Bandar Abbas released his own video, saying his monthly salary of 23 million tomans-about $171-was insufficient to support his family. He openly shared his bank details and declared he would not retract his comments, warning senior officials that neglecting police livelihoods could erode patience within the ranks. Other anonymous clips have since circulated, alleging that retraction videos are coerced and describing the situation as "fire under the ashes." Social media users and moderate outlets have characterized the trend as evidence of attrition and a "silent crisis" inside Iran's security institutions. Reports on Telegram channels have amplified the issue, publishing alleged pay slips showing salaries as low as $119 a month for experienced officers. Analysts say the disclosures highlight growing discontent among police, who lack the housing and benefits often extended to the Revolutionary Guards, underscoring deepening strains within Iran's armed forces. Iran's clerical establishment enters 2026 weakened but intact, with analysts cautioning that Tehran is interpreting survival after a punishing year as justification for taking bigger risks ahead. The past 12 months saw direct confrontation with Israel, clashes with the United States, and the erosion of Iran's regional proxy network. Domestic pressures mounted as inflation and dissent spread, yet the system did not collapse. Instead, experts say the regime is reframing endurance as proof of resilience. Avi Melamed, a Middle East analyst, described a psychological shift inside Iran, noting that "many Iranian people are no longer afraid of this regime." Historian Shahram Kholdi argued that leaders view 2025 through a survivalist lens, encouraging defiance rather than restraint. Journalist Jay Solomon called the year a "reversal," pointing to weakened proxies and economic strain. Still, Iran's missile arsenal remains largely intact, and officials appear determined to rebuild deterrence. Analysts warn that Tehran's belief it "didn't lose" the June war with Israel could heighten the risk of miscalculation in 2026. Survival, they suggest, is being treated as victory - a mindset that may make the coming year more volatile than the last. Irish Senator Sharon Keogan publicly expressed solidarity with Iranians demonstrating against their government, responding to exiled Crown Prince Reza Pahlavi on social media. "Support for you by many Iranians in Ireland for your return. I completely support the brave Iranian men and women taking to the streets to protest. The Ayatollahs' days are numbered," Keogan wrote, adding a message in Persian: "Fight Iran." Iranian parliamentary speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf issued a sharp warning to Washington in a social media post, accusing foreign intelligence services of attempting to turn recent protests by merchants and guilds into armed clashes. "The devil's scream rose high because the efforts of armed field agents of intelligence services...were thwarted by the historical awareness of the Iranian nation," Ghalibaf wrote, translated from Persian. He emphasized that demonstrators should not be equated with "foreign mercenaries," describing them instead as "our dear children." Turning his remarks toward the United States, Ghalibaf said that any admission of involvement would make "all American centers and forces across the entire region legitimate targets" for Iran. He added that Iranians remain united in confronting external threats. The Pezeshkian government has revived a long-delayed push to unify Iran's exchange rates, dismantling a multi-tier system that economists say fostered corruption and uncertainty. On Thursday, the president announced the elimination of the subsidized rate of 285,000 rials per dollar for basic imports, promising direct consumer subsidies instead. Officials argue the reform will reduce rent-seeking and stabilize markets, but analysts caution that inflationary expectations often resurface quickly in an economy burdened by sanctions and fiscal shortfalls. In candid remarks, Pezeshkian admitted dissatisfaction was directed at the state itself, saying, "There is no need to look for America to blame." Protesters, however, appear unconvinced, with slogans targeting the supreme leader and theocratic system rather than exchange-rate policy. The reforms may ease technical distortions, but the crisis has become political in ways economic fixes alone cannot resolve. President Masoud Pezeshkian's decision to reappoint Abdolnaser Hemmati as head of the Central Bank has sparked fierce backlash from hardline lawmakers, exposing divisions within Iran's political establishment. Hemmati, a centrist figure ousted last year after parliament impeached him over exchange-rate volatility, now returns to a post that bypasses legislative approval. "This explicitly ignores parliament's vote," said Tehran lawmaker Zeynab Gheisari, accusing the government of disregarding the will of representatives. Others warned the move showed contempt for the country's economic stability. Hemmati pledged to prioritize inflation control and tighter banking oversight, but his comeback highlights the government's reliance on familiar technocrats even as public anger targets the broader system. The appointment may deepen mistrust between reformists and hardliners at a moment of heightened unrest. A sharp plunge in Iran's currency has reignited protests across the country, with demonstrators venting frustration not only at economic hardship but at the political system itself. The U.S. dollar briefly surged to 1.45 million rials on the open market, accelerating inflation and eroding household purchasing power. "Protesting the dollar is protesting instability," journalist Mustafa Danandeh wrote in Ettelaat, capturing the sense of uncertainty that has left many Iranians unable to plan for rent, medicine, or jobs. Israeli National Security Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir expressed solidarity with demonstrators in Iran, posting a message on social media that was translated from Persian. "The people of Iran deserve a free life, liberated from the killer dictator, Khamenei. We stand with you!" he wrote. President Donald Trump sharply escalated his rhetoric toward Tehran on Friday, warning that the United States would intervene if Iranian authorities used lethal force against demonstrators. His remarks, posted on Truth Social, came after reports of fatalities during clashes between protesters and security forces in the western city of Lordegan. "If Iran violently kills peaceful protesters, which is their custom, the United States of America will come to their rescue," Trump wrote. "We are locked and loaded and ready to go." The statement marked one of the most direct threats of U.S. involvement in Iran's internal unrest in recent years. It also followed months of heightened tensions, including American airstrikes on Iranian nuclear facilities last summer and Trump's pledge this week to support Israeli military action should Iran advance its missile and nuclear programs. Iranian officials quickly condemned the comments. Ali Larijani, head of the country's top security body, accused Trump of "adventurism" and warned that U.S. interference would destabilize the region and damage American interests. Iranian-born Belgian politician Darya Safai has posted a video of support for the protesters in which she said "this must be our last battle." Safai, who has born in Tehran and has been a member of the Belgian Chamber of Representatives for the New Flemish Alliance since 2019 said on X that "the eyes of the world are on Iran." She said that the protestors had "earned the respect of the world" and that their push for a national secular government allied with the West offered an alternative that would guarantee regional security. "You will have their support, but above all, we must know that the architects of that country's future are we, the people of Iran," she said, according to a translation, adding, "we will have a bright future." Mourners have showed defiance at the funerals of protestors who have been killed in the unrest, it has been reported. Those attending the service for Amirhessam Khodayarifard, who was killed in Kuhdasht, chanted "this is the final battle, Pahlavi will return." This referred to exiled opposition leader Reza Pahlavi, who is son of the last shah of Iran, ousted in the 1979 revolution that ushered in the Islamic Republic. There were also anti-government chants shouted at the funeral of Dariush Ansari Bakhtiarvand, a protester who was killed in Isfahan's Fooladshahr, according to Iran International, citing social media channels. At the burial there were chants of "Death to Khamenei" referring to Iran's Supreme Leader, according to reports from the scene. Meanwhile, mourners attending the funeral of Amirhossein Khodayari-Fard, who died during protests in Kuhdasht, chanted slogans directly addressing government forces present at the ceremony, shouting "Basijis, Guards, you are the ISIS," comparing Iran's security forces with Islamist militants. A representative for the Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei has alleged that footage of protests had been fabricated. Ahmad Alamolhoda, who is Khamenei's representative in Razavi Khorasa province said that Israeli media and what he described as "enemy-affiliated outlets" film crowds and used AI technology to overlay anti-government chants. The aim was to create the impression that Iranians had rejected the Islamic Revolution, he added, according to news outlet Iran International. His comments come as protests which started last week continued into Friday with people taking to the streets in Zahedan, Sistan and Baluchistan province in which chants of "death to the dictator" were heard, referring to Khamenei. Meanwhile, mourners attending the funeral of Amirhossein Khodayari-Fard, who was killed during protests in Kuhdasht, chanted slogans at government forces at the ceremony. Trump's warning about intervention in Iran comes days after the Iranian foreign minister called for dialogue with the U.S. In an op-ed for The Guardian , Tehran's top diplomat, Abbas Araghchi wrote that Israeli prime minister Benjamin Netanyahu had "achieved his dream of dragging the U.S. into a military confrontation with Iran which came at "a steep and unprecedented cost for Israel." But more Americans were realizing that Israel was not an ally but a liability and that there could allow "new whole new relationships that may transform our region," he said. Describing a "manufactured crisis over Iran's nuclear program" which he insisted was for peaceful purposes, Araghchi said that regarding its atomic capabilities," the world should also know that Iranians will never abandon their rights. "The U.S. administration now faces a dilemma: it can continue writing blank cheques for Israel with American taxpayer dollars and credibility, or be part of a tectonic change for the better," Aragchi added. The U.S. State Department has said the demonstrations were an expression of anger at the government in Tehran, according to independent news outlet Iran International. "The protests reflect the understandable anger of the Iranian people at their government's failures and excuses," the State Department said in a written statement on Thursday, according to the channel. The outlet said that the statement added that Tehran has neglected the country's economy, agriculture, water, and electricity "for decades" to "squander billions on terrorist proxies and nuclear weapons research." Newsweek has contacted the State Department for further comment outside office hours on Friday. There has been a further response from Tehran to President Trump's warning of U.S. intervention in the protests, this time from Ali Shamkhani, who is a member of Iran's Supreme National Defense Council. In a post on X, Shamkhani said that "any intervening hand that approaches Iran's security will be cut off with a regret-inducing response," according to a translation, run by Iran International, an independent Iranian news outlet. "The people of Iran are well familiar with the experience of "rescuing" Americans; from Iraq and Afghanistan to Gaza," Shamkhani wrote on X, adding that his country's national security was "a red line." Republican congressman Don Bacon has expressed his backing for the protesters in Iran. In a post on X, he said, the Nebraska representative said that that "the vast majority of Iranians want freedom, a government that respects individual liberties and a country that is at peace with its neighbors." "The Iranian people have been abused by their own government for way too long," his post added next to a map detailing where the protests are taking place. Ali Larijani, who is the Secretary of Supreme National Security Council of Iran has responded to Trump's Truth Social post in which he said that the U.S. was "locked and loaded" to help the demonstrators. In a post on X, Larijani said that the government in Tehran considers the concerns of protesters "separate from those of the destructive elements." "Trump should know that American interference in this internal issue is equivalent to chaos across the entire region and the destruction of American interests," added the post, according to a translation. "The American people should know that it was Trump who started the adventurism. They should look after their soldiers." Senator Ted Cruz has said that the protests in Iran show how its government has been weakened by Israel's 12‑day campaign and President Donald Trump's move to destroy its nuclear program. "Losing wars has consequences. Israel's 12‑day campaign against the Iranian regime & President Trump's historic decision to destroy its nuclear program have exposed the regime's weakness to the Iranian people & the world," the Republican Texas senator posted on X As he added that "the Ayatollah's days are numbered." "The Iranian people are rising up & the Ayatollah's days are numbered," he added. Exiled Iranian opposition leader, Reza Pahlavi has praised protesters across Iran as demonstrations entered their fifth day, saying that they are "making history." Pahlavi, the son of the last shah of Iran who was ousted in the 1979 revolution, posted on X that those killed in the demonstrations sparked by economic woes and the collapse of the currency, the rial. He listed provinces from Lorestan and Chaharmahal and Bakhtiari to Tehran, Khorasan and Hormozgan where demonstrations have taken place. "You are making history - history written by the courage, solidarity, and determination of a nation reclaiming its country," wrote Pahlavi. "I call on all compatriots to honor each and every fallen hero of this national uprising-not only in their own cities and regions, but across Iran and around the world," he wrote, "stay united. Stay focused on the goal. Victory will be ours." It follows a New Year's message in which he said that "the current regime has reached the end of the road" and was "at its most fragile: weak, deeply divided, and unable to suppress the courage of a rising nation."

Iran International said that seven protesters were killed by security forces, making Thursday the deadliest day of the protests so far. Opposition group National Council of Resistance of Iran (NCRI) told Fox News Digital in a statement that protests and street clashes continued Thursday morning in Tehran and in cities including Marvdasht, Kermanshah, Delfan and Arak, and claimed that two protesters were killed by direct fire in Lordegan. President Donald Trump and other administration officials voiced support for the demonstrators this week. Speaking Monday, Trump pointed to Iran's economic collapse and long-standing public discontent, while stopping short of explicitly calling for regime change. Maryam Rajavi, the President-elect of the National Council of Resistance of Iran, issued a statement on the continuing protests, noting: "The four-day uprising by merchants, students, and other sectors of society signals the Iranian people's determination to be free from religious tyranny.
